info.name: Gamified Tracker API
info.description:
Covers the API Gateway's public surface (port 8080), plus direct-hit folders for Activity Service (8081) and Gamification Service (8082) for internal debugging, per API.md. Includes a dedicated 'Security - IDOR Verification' folder exercising the userId-from-JWT fix (spoofed header neutralization, intentionally-open reads).

Run order matters: use Collection Runner top-to-bottom (Auth -> Activity -> Activity Log -> Level Tracker -> Ranks -> Activity Level Threshold -> Security -> Misc -> Rate Limiting -> internal-service folders). The 'Ranks' folder depends on 'Level Tracker' having logged XP for User A earlier in the same run. The 'Rate Limiting' folder self-loops a single request (via setNextRequest) to trip the Gateway's 429, so it only behaves correctly under the Collection Runner, not a manual single Send. Test data (emails, activity name, a synthetic activityId) is generated once per run by the collection-level pre-request script and cached in collection variables, so re-running the whole collection produces fresh, non-colliding data each time.

Prerequisites: `docker-compose up --build` (or the equivalent Maven/local run) so the Gateway (8080), Activity Service (8081), Gamification Service (8082), and Eureka are all up.

Known caveats baked into these tests:
- `POST /api/activitylog` (and its internal-service equivalent) can 500 due to a pre-existing, unrelated bug in `ActivityLogServiceImpl`'s XP-bonus roll (`RandomGenerator.getDefault()` failing to find the `L32X64MixRandom` algorithm provider in some JVM/container images). If that request fails, downstream requests that depend on its output (`activityLogId`) are guarded to report a skip rather than crash the run.
- No-token requests currently return `403`, not the semantically-expected `401` (default Spring Security behavior with no custom `AuthenticationEntryPoint`) - tests accept either.
- `GET /level/{id}` and `GET /activitylog/{id}` reads are intentionally open to any authenticated user (a social/leaderboard feature, not an access-control gap) - tests assert `200`, not `403`, when a different user reads them.

exec: // Bootstrap unique test data once per collection run.

request.description: Public endpoint. Registers an ADMIN user (role is client-suppliable at register time - a known, documented tradeoff, not something this collection needs to work around).

exec: pm.test('Response body is a JWT (3 dot-separated parts)', function () {

request.description: Registers a plain USER (role omitted, defaults to USER). Decodes the returned JWT to capture the numeric userId claim used throughout the rest of the collection.

exec: pm.test('Token contains a userId claim', function () {

request.description: A second independent user, used throughout the Security folder to prove one user cannot write data as another, and that reads are intentionally open across users.

exec: pm.test('Token contains a userId claim', function () {

request.description: Confirms login works independently of register and returns a token for the same identity.

exec: pm.test('Token subject matches the login email', function () {
pm.test('Token userId matches the userId captured at registration', function () {

request.description: Wrong password for a real account. Must fail with the same message as a nonexistent-account login, so the error never reveals which one was wrong.

exec: pm.test('ProblemDetail does not reveal whether the email or password was wrong', function () {

request.description: ADMIN-only. `createdAt` is accepted but always server-set, so it's omitted here.
A plain USER token must be rejected - admin gating is enforced at the URL level in SecurityConfig.

exec: pm.test('Contains the activity created earlier', function () {

item.name: Get Activity By Name - Not Found

request.description:
IMPORTANT: this endpoint can return 500 due to a pre-existing, unrelated bug in ActivityLogServiceImpl's XP-bonus roll (RandomGenerator.getDefault() failing to resolve the L32X64MixRandom algorithm in some JVM/container images). If it fails here, that is a known separate issue, not an IDOR regression - the userId-binding fix is independently verified in the Security folder via POST /api/level, which shares the identical Gateway header-injection mechanism but has no RNG dependency.

Note there is deliberately no userId field in the body - the acting user is derived server-side from the JWT.

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 (see request description for the known unrelated failure mode)', function () {
pm.test('Log is owned by the authenticated caller (User A), not any client-supplied value', function () {
console.warn('Create Activity Log failed - downstream requests depending on activityLogId will be skipped.');
pm.test.skip('Skipped - no activityLogId (Create Activity Log likely failed, see that request)');

item.name: Get Activity Log By Id (Different User - Open Read)

request.description: User B reading User A's log by id. This must succeed - reads are intentionally open to any authenticated user (a social feature), not gated by ownership.

exec: pm.test.skip('Skipped - no activityLogId (Create Activity Log likely failed, see that request)');
pm.test('Status is 200 (open read by design, not 403)', function () {

Get All Logs For User (Open Read, by User B)

request.description: User B listing User A's logs. Open read by design.

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 (open read by design, not 403)', function () {

exec: pm.test('XP is credited to the authenticated caller', function () {

item.name: Get Level Tracker By Id - Not Found

request.description: LevelTrackerDto never exposes its internal numeric id (only userId/activityId/level/totalXp/currentLevelXp), so a happy-path GET /api/level/{id} isn't reachable from any other endpoint's response in this API surface - only the not-found case is scriptable without direct DB access.

item.name: Get Level Trackers By User (User A)

exec: pm.test('Contains the row written earlier', function () {
pm.test('Response is a non-empty array', function () {

item.name: Ranks
item.description: Exercises the overall-level + percentile-rank system (/api/ranks/**). Depends on the 'Level Tracker' folder running first in the same collection run, so User A has XP on record before recompute is triggered. Rank tiers are relative/percentile-based across ALL users ever tracked in this database, so exact tier/percentile values are not asserted here - only response shape and internal consistency (e.g. distribution counts, tier membership).

item.name: Get My Rank - Not Yet Ranked (404)

request.description: User A was just registered this run and has never been through a recompute, so no UserRank snapshot exists yet for this specific userId.

exec: pm.test('Status is 404 (no snapshot until the first recompute)', function () {

request.description: Not admin-gated in the current SecurityConfig - any authenticated user can trigger it. Re-ranks every user with LevelTracker rows; normally runs on a schedule (ranking.recompute-interval-ms), this is the on-demand equivalent for testing/demos.

exec: pm.test('rankedUsers is at least 1 (User A has logged XP earlier in this run)', function () {

request.description: After the recompute, User A has a snapshot. Captures the assigned tier into a collection variable for the leaderboard requests below.

exec: pm.test('tier is one of the 9 Summit ladder tiers', function () {
pm.test('totalUsers is at least position (position cannot exceed the population size)', function () {

request.description: Resolves User A's own tier server-side and returns that tier's leaderboard - User A must appear in it.

exec: pm.test('User A appears in their own tier leaderboard', function () {

request.description: Same tier as the previous request, addressed directly by name (as any client would to browse a specific tier) rather than resolved from the caller's own rank.

exec: pm.test('Response is a non-empty array', function () {
pm.test('Every entry reports the requested tier', function () {
pm.test('withinRankPosition is 1-based and starts at 1 on the first page', function () {

request.description: One row per RankTier, including tiers with zero users - useful for a 'population by altitude' dashboard chart.

exec: pm.test('User A\'s tier has at least 1 user', function () {
var myTier = json.find(function (d) { return d.tier === pm.collectionVariables.get('userATier'); });

request.description: Not admin-gated in the current SecurityConfig - any authenticated user can create/overwrite a threshold.
A read despite the POST verb - the body is used purely as a lookup key, not persisted.

exec: pm.test('xpRequired matches the threshold created earlier', function () {

item.name: Write XP As Self (No Spoofed Header)

request.description: Baseline: a normal write with no userId in the body and no manually-set userId header. The Gateway must inject User A's id from the JWT.

exec: pm.test('Credited to the authenticated caller (User A)', function () {

item.name: Write XP With Forged userId Header (Spoof Neutralized)

request.description: The core IDOR regression test: authenticated as User A, but the request manually forges a 'userId' header pointing at User B. JwtFilter must overwrite this header before the request is routed downstream, so the write still lands on User A, never User B.

exec: pm.test('Forged header is ignored - XP is still credited to User A, the authenticated caller', function () {
pm.test('XP was NOT credited to User B, the spoofed target', function () {

item.name: Read Another User's Level Data (Open Read By Design)

request.description: User A reading User B's level/XP data. This is intentionally allowed - a social/leaderboard feature, not an access-control gap - so this must be 200, not 403.

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 (open read by design)', function () {

item.name: Read Another User's Activity Logs (Open Read By Design)

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 (open read by design)', function () {

request.description: No Authorization header at all. Currently returns 403 (Spring Security's default anonymous-auth behavior, no custom AuthenticationEntryPoint) rather than the semantically-expected 401 - this is pre-existing and unrelated to the IDOR fix, so the test accepts either.

exec: pm.test('Status is 401 or 403 (unauthenticated request rejected)', function () {
pm.test('Greets the authenticated caller by email', function () {

item.name: Rate Limiting
item.description:
Exercises the Gateway's Redis-backed Bucket4j rate limiter. Fires the same proxied request (GET /api/level, on the gamification route) repeatedly as one authenticated user until the per-user token bucket is exhausted and the Gateway returns 429 with an X-RateLimit-Remaining header.

The single request self-loops via postman.setNextRequest, so this only works in the Collection Runner / Newman - a plain single Send fires it just once. The limit is keyed on the JWT-derived userId (IP fallback), so it throttles only User A's own bucket; it does not touch other users or the separate /auth guard.

Caveats: earlier folders also made /api/level, /api/threshold and /api/notifications calls as User A (the same bucket), so the 429 can appear before a full 100 requests - the loop just runs until it trips, capped at 130 attempts. On rapid re-runs within the same 60s window the bucket may still be partly drained from the previous run (it refills at capacity/period).

request.description: Self-looping request: keeps re-sending itself (postman.setNextRequest) until the Gateway rate limiter answers 429, then asserts the 429 and its X-RateLimit-Remaining header. Run via the Collection Runner - setNextRequest is a no-op on a single Send, so a manual Send will just return 200.

exec: pm.test('Rate limiter returns 429 once the per-user limit is exceeded', function () {
pm.test('429 response carries an X-RateLimit-Remaining header', function () {
console.log('Rate limit tripped after ' + attempts + ' request(s) in this run.');
pm.test('A 429 is observed before the ' + MAX_ATTEMPTS + '-request cap', function () {
pm.expect.fail('No 429 after ' + MAX_ATTEMPTS + ' requests - is rate limiting enabled and Redis reachable?');
// Still under the limit (expect 200) - loop back and fire the same request again.
console.warn('Unexpected pre-limit status ' + pm.response.code + ' on attempt ' + attempts);

item.name: Activity Service (Internal :8081)
item.description: Direct calls to the Activity Service, bypassing the Gateway - reachable in this dev setup since there's no network isolation yet. This service has no security layer of its own: no auth is enforced, and the 'userId' header (used by POST /activitylog/) is entirely self-reported here since there's no JWT to derive it from. Only trustworthy when it arrives via the Gateway.

item.name: Create Activity Log (Direct, Manual userId Header)

request.description: Same known RNG-bug caveat as the Gateway equivalent in the Activity Log folder applies here.

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 (see request description for the known unrelated failure mode)', function () {

item.name: Get Activity Log By Id - Not Found (Direct)
Gamification Service (Internal :8082)
item.description: Direct calls to the Gamification Service, bypassing the Gateway. No security layer of its own - the 'userId' header (used by POST /level) is self-reported here, only trustworthy when it arrives via the Gateway or activity-service's internal Feign call.

exec: pm.test('userId in response matches the manually-supplied header', function () {

request.description: Depends on 'Create Threshold' in the Activity Level Threshold folder having run earlier in the same collection run (folders execute top-to-bottom). Accepts 404 too, in case this folder is run in isolation.

exec: pm.test('Status is 200 or 404 (200 if the Activity Level Threshold folder already ran this collection run)', function () {
